pattern: Resolve the two trivial cases directly (same building; the taller one is already reachable), then answer every remaining query offline with a single left-to-right scan: buildings feed a shared min-heap of pending queries keyed by the height they still need to beat, so the first building tall enough to satisfy a pending query is, by scan order, automatically the leftmost one.
hints: Two cases need no search at all: if a query's two buildings are the same, that is the answer; if one building is already shorter than the other, they can meet at the taller one directly (since the shorter one can move straight there).

For every remaining ('hard') query, what you actually need is the leftmost building to the right of both indices that is taller than the taller of the two -- attach that requirement to the larger of the two indices.

Scan buildings left to right with a min-heap of pending requirements (keyed by the height threshold they need beaten): before adding a building's own newly-pending queries to the heap, first pop and resolve every entry whose threshold this building's height already beats -- the current index is then guaranteed to be the smallest one that works.

edge_inputs.description: the two buildings are already the same one
the shorter of the pair is queried first (unordered indices)
no taller building exists anywhere to the right
strictly increasing heights, every adjacent pair meets one step to the right
several queries share the same rightmost index and must be resolved in the right order
all buildings the same height, nobody can ever meet except at themselves

stress.description: random heights with a batch of random-pair queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 3000))
    heights = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    qn = max(1, min(size, 3000))
    queries = [[rng.randint(0, n - 1), rng.randint(0, n - 1)] for _ in range(qn)]
    return [heights, queries]
